text: The surgeon navigates through the surgical field, freeing adhesions from a previous operation. The small bowel anastomosis is identified, and a sizable mesenteric tumor is visible.

The surgeon encounters adhesions resembling an umbilical hernia containing small bowel. The process of freeing these adhesions is underway to better visualize the anatomy and determine the necessary resections.
The surgeon encounters bleeding from the mesentery and discovers an unexpected carcinoid tumor in the small bowel during the procedure.

The surgeon locates the proximal small intestine associated with the mesenteric tumor, maneuvering around the proximal small bowel and beginning dissection through the mesentery to proceed along its length.
The surgeon maneuvers the instruments back and forth, creating a V-shaped dissection around a tumor located at the root of the mesentery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to work on the mesentery, scoring the visceral peritoneum to thin it out and ensure only the vessel and minimal fat are captured between the jaws.

The surgeon continues dissecting down the mesentery on the proximal side of the mesenteric tumor, carefully maneuvering to avoid significant bleeding. The surgeon prepares to flip the tissue to assess the path beneath the tumor.

The surgeon scores the distal aspect of the mesentery and progresses towards the small bowel margin distal to the mesenteric tumor. The decision is made to resect the tumor en bloc with the mesentery, necessitating the removal of the associated small bowel segment.
The surgeon identifies the distal small bowel margin and visually aligns it with previous markings, preparing to connect the tissue planes around the mesenteric tumor.
The surgeon returns to the proximal aspect of the tumor, positioning just below it, and begins dissecting underneath the tumor.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect through thick mesentery underneath the tumor, encountering venous bleeding from the mesentery. The bleeding begins as the surgeon continues the dissection.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to manage a venous bleed in the mesentery. The left hand applies pressure while the right hands prepare with a silk suture, suction in the fourth arm, and a needle driver in the third arm. A figure-of-eight suture is placed to control the bleeding from the wide-open vessel.
The surgeon completes the mesenteric resection using a vessel sealer, carefully excising the remaining mesentery tissue around the tumor.

The surgeon checks the perfusion at the distal margin of the bowel, using blue loads to transect the bowel and white loads to create the anastomosis. The specimen is visible, and the proximal margin is identified.
The surgeon identifies the proximal margin and moves the specimen to the right side of the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon evaluates the proximal margin of the bowel, noting its gray appearance and poor perfusion compared to the distal bowel. Deciding to resect more of the proximal margin, the surgeon prepares for the first small bowel anastomosis of the case.

The surgeon performs an antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 60mm white load, followed by a second isoparastaltic anastomosis. The ICG is used to check perfusion, revealing strong fluorescence in the distal bowel on the left side, while the proximal bowel on the right side shows weaker fluorescence. The common channel is closed with a 3-0 V-Loc suture.
The surgeon inspects the mesentery for any signs of bleeding, confirming that the area looks clear and intact. The operation is completed using minimally invasive techniques.
The surgeon repositions the small bowel and begins to measure the remaining length of the proximal small bowel, running the bowel through their hands.

The surgeon inspects a small spot in the small bowel and identifies a one-centimeter carcinoid tumor approximately thirty centimeters proximal to the anastomosis. The surrounding mesentery is examined, revealing no pathologic lymph nodes or additional masses. The surgeon prepares to perform a limited small bowel resection to remove the tumor.
The surgeon prepares to perform an isoperistaltic cytosine anastomosis on the small bowel, aligning the bowel segments for connection.
